title: Dog Vomiting Clear Liquid: Causes, Triage, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Discover why your dog is vomiting clear liquid, when it becomes a medium-level urgency, and why your vet needs a photo for faster triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Clear liquid vomit in dogs is usually a medium-priority issue involving stomach acid or nausea; seek veterinary care if it is accompanied by lethargy or an inability to keep water down.

What does it mean if my dog is vomiting clear liquid?

When a dog vomits clear liquid, they are often expelling gastric juices or saliva. This usually happens on an empty stomach and can be caused by minor issues like drinking water too fast or more significant concerns like nausea, acid reflux, or early-stage gastrointestinal irritation.

When is clear vomiting in dogs considered an emergency?

  • The urgency level for clear vomiting is currently Medium.
  • While a single episode may not be a crisis if your dog is still active, frequent vomiting or vomiting paired with lethargy requires veterinary attention.
  • If your dog is unable to keep any water down or appears distressed, contact a clinic within 12 to 24 hours.

How does taking a photo of the vomit help my veterinarian?

  • Capturing a photo of the vomit serves as a vital triage tool.
  • It helps your veterinarian identify the exact consistency and check for hidden triggers like foreign material, froth, or slight discoloration that might be missed by the naked eye.
  • This visual evidence allows the medical team to prioritize your dog's care more effectively.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Vomiting of sudden onset can be attributed to dietary indiscretion or intolerance, drug or toxin ingestion (including antibiotics, NSAIDs, corticosteroids, or chemicals), systemic illness (such as pancreatitis or hypoadrenocorticism), endoparasitism, or bacterial or viral infections. Diagnosis is typically based on history, clinical findings, and response to symptomatic treatment. Diagnostic imaging, including abdominal radiographs and ultrasound, may be indicated, along with specific tests such as ACTH stimulation. Treatment for acute vomiting involves discontinuing oral intake for approximately 24 hours, followed by the introduction of small, frequent amounts of a bland, low-fat, easily digestible diet, with a gradual return to the usual diet over 3-5 days. Antiemetic drugs like metoclopramide, maropitant, or ondansetron can be used if vomiting is protracted or severe enough to cause dehydration or electrolyte imbalances, but only after an etiologic diagnosis has been made.

Chapter: Gastroenterology, Toxicology, Infectious Disease

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 387)

faqs.question: How long should I wait before feeding my dog again?
faqs.answer: Usually, a 'stomach rest' of 6 to 12 hours is recommended after vomiting, followed by a bland diet like boiled chicken and white rice, but always consult your vet before restarting food.

faqs.question: Could clear vomit be a sign of bloat?
faqs.answer: Clear liquid or white foam can be a sign of bloat if accompanied by a distended abdomen and unproductive retching; if you see these signs, it is a life-threatening emergency.

faqs.question: Should I withhold water if my dog is vomiting?
faqs.answer: If your dog vomits immediately after drinking, you should limit them to small amounts of water or ice cubes to prevent dehydration without further over-stimulating the stomach.
